text: IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON DEPENDABLE AND SECURE COMPUTING, VOL. 21, NO. 2, MARCH/APRIL 2024 905
Automatically Identifying CVE Affected Versions
With Patches and Developer Logs
Yongzhong He , Yiming Wang , Sencun Zhu , Wei Wang , Yunjia Zhang, Qiang Li , and Aimin Yu
Abstract —While vulnerability databases are important sources

of information for software security, it is known that information in these databases is inconsistent. How to rectify these incorrect data is a challenging issue. In this article, we employ developer logs and patches to automatically identify vulnerable source code versions that each CVE really affects. Our tool organizes all versions of a piece of software into a version tree, and identiﬁes the ﬁrst vulner- able version, and the last vulnerable versions in the version tree trunk and branches. For evaluation, we took Linux Kernel as the case study and quantiﬁed the error rate of the vulnerable versions reported by the NVD. The total number of vulnerable Linux Kernel versions reported by the NVD was 43,727 (as of September 2020), of which the total number of false positives reached 2,497 and the total number of false negatives reached 9,330, accounting for 5.7% and 21.34%, respectively. In addition, we compare our tool with two vulnerability detection tools and show that our tool could achieve high detection accuracy.
Index Terms —Developer logs, NVD, patch, versions, vulnerab-
ility.
CCS CONCEPTS: Security and privacy Software
security

were presently available as of 2020, as reported by Lopes [2] . Therefore, it is important to identify and remedy the software security problems that are constantly found to protect computer systems.
Security professionals rely on various sources of information

to inform them of new security issues. An important source is the vulnerability database. Public vulnerability databases in- clude CVE, NVD, SecurityFocus, Redhat Bugzilla, ExploitDB, OpenWall, SecurityTracker, etc., but we focus on the most widely used and authoritative database: National Vulnerability Database (NVD) [3] in this work. The database is maintained by the U.S. government, committed to accurately record all known vulnerabilities, and effectively as an industry standard. For ex- ample, business security services (such as Hakiri [4] , Snyk [5] and SourceClear [6] ) and open-source security tools (such as BudlerAudit [7] ,OWASPOSSIndex [8] anddependencycheck- ing [9] ) rely on NVD vulnerability information to run effectively. Researchers [10] , [12] use the NVD as the core data source to reveal all aspects of vulnerability discovery and recovery process. Aksu et al. [13] rely on the information of the NVD to generate attack graph automatically. However, is the information from the NVD database accurate? Indeed, previous work has pointed out the inaccuracy of NVD information. For example, Dong et al. [14] reported the inconsistent vulnerable software versions of the NVD are highly prevalent: only 59.82% of the vulnerability CVE summaries strictly match the standardized NVD entries, and the inconsistency level increases over time. Anwar et al. [15] discovered the inconsistent or incomplete data of the NVD and showed the possible impact of these data prob- lems. Other work [16] , [17] , [18] also investigated some types of NVD data quality problems. The above work indicated that the known affected software conﬁguration of NVD (introduced in Section II-A ) was indeed inaccurate. However, the previous work mostly pointed out the inaccuracy of the NVD, with the following question largely remaining unanswered: which vulnerable versions are exactly affected by a CVE-ID?
To ﬁll this gap, we aim to propose a practical solution to

justify the incorrect versions reported by the NVD. For a given CVE, none of the existing vulnerability detection methods can guarantee that the vulnerability can be accurately identiﬁed in all released versions, so just relying on these methods is not enough. For example, patch-based detection [19] , [20] has one assumption: the unpatched code is vulnerable and patched code is secure. However, in this article we ﬁnd this assumption is not correct. Fuzzing [21] has low false positives, but it is difﬁcult to fuzz programs that take highly structured inputs, or
